lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: So, now prosperity begins to mellow

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And drop into the rotten mouth of death.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Here in these confines slily have I lurk’d,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: To watch the waning of mine adversaries.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: A dire induction am I witness to,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And will to France, hoping the consequence

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Will prove as bitter, black, and tragical.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Withdraw thee, wretched Margaret: who comes here?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Ah, my young princes! ah, my tender babes!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: My unblown flowers, new-appearing sweets!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: If yet your gentle souls fly in the air

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And be not fix’d in doom perpetual,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Hover about me with your airy wings

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And hear your mother’s lamentation!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Hover about her; say, that right for right

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Hath dimm’d your infant morn to aged night.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: So many miseries have crazed my voice,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: That my woe-wearied tongue is mute and dumb,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Edward Plantagenet, why art thou dead?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Plantagenet doth quit Plantagenet.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Edward for Edward pays a dying debt.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Wilt thou, O God, fly from such gentle lambs,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And throw them in the entrails of the wolf?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: When didst thou sleep when such a deed was done?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: When holy Harry died, and my sweet son.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Blind sight, dead life, poor mortal living ghost,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Woe’s scene, world’s shame, grave’s due by life usurp’d,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Brief abstract and record of tedious days,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Rest thy unrest on England’s lawful earth, Sitting down.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Unlawfully made drunk with innocents’ blood!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: O, that thou wouldst as well afford a grave

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: As thou canst yield a melancholy seat!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Then would I hide my bones, not rest them here.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: O, who hath any cause to mourn but I? Sitting down by her.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: If ancient sorrow be most reverend,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Give mine the benefit of seniory,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And let my woes frown on the upper hand.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: If sorrow can admit society, Sitting down with them.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Tell o’er your woes again by viewing mine:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: I had an Edward, till a Richard kill’d him;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: I had a Harry, till a Richard kill’d him:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thou hadst an Edward, till a Richard kill’d him;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thou hadst a Richard, till a Richard killed him;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I had a Richard too, and thou didst kill him;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I had a Rutland too, thou holp’st to kill him.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thou hadst a Clarence too, and Richard kill’d him.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: From forth the kennel of thy womb hath crept

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: A hell-hound that doth hunt us all to death:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: That dog, that had his teeth before his eyes,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: To worry lambs and lap their gentle blood,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: That foul defacer of God’s handiwork,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: That excellent grand tyrant of the earth,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: That reigns in galled eyes of weeping souls,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thy womb let loose, to chase us to our graves.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: O upright, just, and true-disposing God,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: How do I thank thee, that this carnal cur

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Preys on the issue of his mother’s body,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And makes her pew-fellow with others’ moan!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: O Harry’s wife, triumph not in my woes!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: God witness with me, I have wept for thine.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Bear with me; I am hungry for revenge,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And now I cloy me with beholding it.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thy Edward he is dead, that stabb’d my Edward;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thy other Edward dead, to quit my Edward;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Young York he is but boot, because both they

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Match not the high perfection of my loss:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thy Clarence he is dead that kill’d my Edward;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And the beholders of this tragic play,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: The adulterate Hastings, Rivers, Vaughan, Grey,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Untimely smother’d in their dusky graves.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Richard yet lives, hell’s black intelligencer,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Only reserved their factor, to buy souls

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And send them thither: but at hand, at hand,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Ensues his piteous and unpitied end:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Earth gapes, hell burns, fiends roar, saints pray,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: To have him suddenly convey’d away.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Cancel his bond of life, dear God, I pray,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: That I may live to say, The dog is dead!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: O, thou didst prophesy the time would come

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: That I should wish for thee to help me curse

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: That bottled spider, that foul bunch-back’d toad!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: I call’d thee then vain flourish of my fortune;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: I call’d thee then poor shadow, painted queen;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: The presentation of but what I was;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: The flattering index of a direful pageant;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: One heaved a-high, to be hurl’d down below;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: A mother only mock’d with two sweet babes;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: A dream of what thou wert, a breath, a bubble,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: A sign of dignity, a garish flag,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: To be the aim of every dangerous shot;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: A queen in jest, only to fill the scene.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Where is thy husband now? where be thy brothers?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Where are thy children? wherein dost thou joy?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Who sues to thee and cries “God save the queen”?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Where be the bending peers that flatter’d thee?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Where be the thronging troops that follow’d thee?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Decline all this, and see what now thou art:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: For happy wife, a most distressed widow;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: For joyful mother, one that wails the name;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: For queen, a very caitiff crown’d with care;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: For one being sued to, one that humbly sues;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: For one that scorn’d at me, now scorn’d of me;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: For one being fear’d of all, now fearing one;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: For one commanding all, obey’d of none.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thus hath the course of justice wheel’d about,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And left thee but a very prey to time;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Having no more but thought of what thou wert,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: To torture thee the more, being what thou art.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thou didst usurp my place, and dost thou not

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Usurp the just proportion of my sorrow?

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Now thy proud neck bears half my burthen’d yoke;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: From which even here I slip my weary neck,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And leave the burthen of it all on thee.

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Farewell, York’s wife, and queen of sad mischance:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: These English woes will make me smile in France.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: O thou well skill’d in curses, stay awhile,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And teach me how to curse mine enemies!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Forbear to sleep the nights, and fast the days;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Compare dead happiness with living woe;

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Think that thy babes were fairer than they were,

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: And he that slew them fouler than he is:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Bettering thy loss makes the bad causer worse:

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Revolving this will teach thee how to curse.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: My words are dull; O, quicken them with thine!

lines.speaker: QUEEN MARGARET
lines.text: Thy woes will make them sharp, and pierce like mine.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Why should calamity be full of words?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Windy attorneys to their client woes,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Airy succeeders of intestate joys,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Poor breathing orators of miseries!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Let them have scope: though what they do impart

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Help not all, yet do they ease the heart.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: If so, then be not tongue-tied: go with me,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And in the breath of bitter words let’s smother

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: My damned son, which thy two sweet sons smother’d.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I hear his drum: be copious in exclaims.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Who intercepts my expedition?

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: O, she that might have intercepted thee,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: By strangling thee in her accursed womb,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: From all the slaughters, wretch, that thou hast done!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Hidest thou that forehead with a golden crown,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Where should be graven, if that right were right,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The slaughter of the prince that owed that crown,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And the dire death of my two sons and brothers?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Tell me, thou villain slave, where are my children?

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Thou toad, thou toad, where is thy brother Clarence?

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And little Ned Plantagenet, his son?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Where is kind Hastings, Rivers, Vaughan, Grey?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: A flourish, trumpets! strike alarum, drums!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Let not the heavens hear these tell-tale women

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Rail on the Lord’s enointed: strike, I say!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Either be patient, and entreat me fair,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Or with the clamorous report of war

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Thus will I drown your exclamations.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Art thou my son?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Ay, I thank God, my father, and yourself.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Then patiently hear my impatience.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Madam, I have a touch of your condition,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Which cannot brook the accent of reproof.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: O, let me speak!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Do then; but I’ll not hear.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I will be mild and gentle in my speech.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And brief, good mother; for I am in haste.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Art thou so hasty? I have stay’d for thee,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: God knows, in anguish, pain and agony.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And came I not at last to comfort you?

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: No, by the holy rood, thou know’st it well,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Thou camest on earth to make the earth my hell.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: A grievous burthen was thy birth to me;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Tetchy and wayward was thy infancy;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Thy school-days frightful, desperate, wild, and furious,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Thy prime of manhood daring, bold, and venturous,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Thy age confirm’d, proud, subdued, bloody, treacherous,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: More mild, but yet more harmful, kind in hatred:

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: What comfortable hour canst thou name,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: That ever graced me in thy company?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Faith, none, but Humphrey Hour, that call’d your grace

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To breakfast once forth of my company.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: If I be so disgracious in your sight,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Let me march on, and not offend your grace.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Strike the drum.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: I prithee, hear me speak.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: You speak too bitterly.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Hear me a word;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: For I shall never speak to thee again.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: So.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Either thou wilt die, by God’s just ordinance,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Ere from this war thou turn a conqueror,

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Or I with grief and extreme age shall perish

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And never look upon thy face again.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Therefore take with thee my most heavy curse;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Which, in the day of battle, tire thee more

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Than all the complete armour that thou wear’st!

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: My prayers on the adverse party fight;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And there the little souls of Edward’s children

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Whisper the spirits of thine enemies

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: And promise them success and victory.

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Bloody thou art, bloody will be thy end;

lines.speaker: DUCHESS
lines.text: Shame serves thy life and doth thy death attend.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Though far more cause, yet much less spirit to curse

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Abides in me; I say amen to all.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Stay, madam; I must speak a word with you.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: I have no more sons of the royal blood

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: For thee to murder: for my daughters, Richard,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: They shall be praying nuns, not weeping queens;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And therefore level not to hit their lives.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: You have a daughter call’d Elizabeth,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Virtuous and fair, royal and gracious.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And must she die for this? O, let her live,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And I’ll corrupt her manners, stain her beauty;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Slander myself as false to Edward’s bed;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Throw over her the veil of infamy:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: So she may live unscarr’d of bleeding slaughter,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: I will confess she was not Edward’s daughter.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Wrong not her birth, she is of royal blood.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: To save her life, I’ll say she is not so.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Her life is only safest in her birth.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And only in that safety died her brothers.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Lo, at their births good stars were opposite.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: No, to their lives bad friends were contrary.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: All unavoided is the doom of destiny.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: True, when avoided grace makes destiny:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: My babes were destined to a fairer death,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: If grace had bless’d thee with a fairer life.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: You speak as if that I had slain my cousins.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Cousins, indeed; and by their uncle cozen’d

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Of comfort, kingdom, kindred, freedom, life.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Whose hand soever lanced their tender hearts,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Thy head, all indirectly, gave direction:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: No doubt the murderous knife was dull and blunt

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Till it was whetted on thy stone-hard heart,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: To revel in the entrails of my lambs.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: But that still use of grief makes wild grief tame,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: My tongue should to thy ears not name my boys

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Till that my nails were anchor’d in thine eyes;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And I, in such a desperate bay of death,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Like a poor bark, of sails and tackling reft,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Rush all to pieces on thy rocky bosom.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Madam, so thrive I in my enterprise

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And dangerous success of bloody wars,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: As I intend more good to you and yours

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Than ever you or yours were by me wrong’d!

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: What good is cover’d with the face of heaven,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: To be discover’d, that can do me good?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The advancement of your children, gentle lady.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Up to some scaffold, there to lose their heads?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: No, to the dignity and height of honour,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The high imperial type of this earth’s glory.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Flatter my sorrows with report of it;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Tell me what state, what dignity, what honour,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Canst thou demise to any child of mine?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Even all I have; yea, and myself and all,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Will I withal endow a child of thine;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: So in the Lethe of thy angry soul

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Thou drown the sad remembrance of those wrongs

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Which thou supposest I have done to thee.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Be brief, lest that be process of thy kindness

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Last longer telling than thy kindness’ date.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Then know, that from my soul I love thy daughter.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: My daughter’s mother thinks it with her soul.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: What do you think?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: That thou dost love my daughter from thy soul:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: So from thy soul’s love didst thou love her brothers;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And from my heart’s love I do thank thee for it.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Be not so hasty to confound my meaning:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I mean, that with my soul I love thy daughter,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And mean to make her queen of England.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Say then, who dost thou mean shall be her king?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Even he that makes her queen: who should be else?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: What, thou?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I, even I: what think you of it, madam?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: How canst thou woo her?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: That would I learn of you,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: As one that are best acquainted with her humour.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And wilt thou learn of me?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Madam, with all my heart.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Send to her, by the man that slew her brothers,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: A pair of bleeding-hearts; thereon engrave

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Edward and York; then haply she will weep:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Therefore present to her—as sometime Margaret

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Did to thy father, steep’d in Rutland’s blood—

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: A handkerchief; which, say to her, did drain

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The purple sap from her sweet brother’s body.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And bid her dry her weeping eyes therewith.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: If this inducement force her not to love,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Send her a story of thy noble acts;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Tell her thou madest away her uncle Clarence,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Her uncle Rivers; yea, and, for her sake,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Madest quick conveyance with her good aunt Anne.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Come, come, you mock me; this is not the way

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To win our daughter.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: There is no other way;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Unless thou couldst put on some other shape,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And not be Richard that hath done all this.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Say that I did all this for love of her.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Nay, then indeed she cannot choose but hate thee,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Having bought love with such a bloody spoil.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Look, what is done cannot be now amended:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Men shall deal unadvisedly sometimes,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Which after hours give leisure to repent.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: If I did take the kingdom from your sons,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To make amends, Ill give it to your daughter.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: If I have kill’d the issue of your womb,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To quicken your increase, I will beget

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Mine issue of your blood upon your daughter:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: A grandam’s name is little less in love

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Than is the doting title of a mother;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: They are as children but one step below,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Even of your mettle, of your very blood;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Of all one pain, save for a night of groans

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Endured of her, for whom you bid like sorrow.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Your children were vexation to your youth,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: But mine shall be a comfort to your age.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The loss you have is but a son being king,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And by that loss your daughter is made queen.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I cannot make you what amends I would,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Therefore accept such kindness as I can.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Dorset your son, that with a fearful soul

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Leads discontented steps in foreign soil,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: This fair alliance quickly shall call home

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To high promotions and great dignity:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The king, that calls your beauteous daughter wife,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Familiarly shall call thy Dorset brother;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Again shall you be mother to a king,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And all the ruins of distressful times

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Repair’d with double riches of content.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: What! we have many goodly days to see:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The liquid drops of tears that you have shed

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Shall come again, transform’d to orient pearl,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Advantaging their loan with interest

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Of ten times double gain of happiness.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Go, then my mother, to thy daughter go;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Make bold her bashful years with your experience;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Prepare her ears to hear a wooer’s tale;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Put in her tender heart the aspiring flame

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Of golden sovereignty; acquaint the princess

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: With the sweet silent hours of marriage joys:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And when this arm of mine hath chastised

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The petty rebel, dull-brain’d Buckingham,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Bound with triumphant garlands will I come

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And lead thy daughter to a conqueror’s bed;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To whom I will retail my conquest won,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And she shall be sole victress, Caesar’s Caesar.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: What were I best to say? her father’s brother

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Would be her lord? or shall I say, her uncle?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Or, he that slew her brothers and her uncles?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Under what title shall I woo for thee,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: That God, the law, my honour and her love,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Can make seem pleasing to her tender years?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Infer fair England’s peace by this alliance.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Which she shall purchase with still lasting war.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Say that the king, which may command, entreats.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: That at her hands which the king’s King forbids.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Say, she shall be a high and mighty queen.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: To wail the tide, as her mother doth.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Say, I will love her everlastingly.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: But how long shall that title “ever” last?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Sweetly in force unto her fair life’s end.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: But how long fairly shall her sweet life last?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: So long as heaven and nature lengthens it.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: So long as hell and Richard likes of it.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Say, I, her sovereign, am her subject love.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: But she, your subject, loathes such sovereignty.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Be eloquent in my behalf to her.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: An honest tale speeds best being plainly told.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Then in plain terms tell her my loving tale.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Plain and not honest is too harsh a style.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Your reasons are too shallow and too quick.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: O no, my reasons are too deep and dead;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Too deep and dead, poor infants, in their grave.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Harp not on that string, madam; that is past.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Harp on it still shall I till heart-strings break.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Now, by my George, my garter, and my crown—

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Profaned, dishonour’d, and the third usurp’d.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I swear—

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: By nothing; for this is no oath:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The George, profaned, hath lost his holy honour;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The garter, blemish’d, pawn’d his knightly virtue;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The crown, usurp’d, disgraced his kingly glory.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: If something thou wilt swear to be believed,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Swear then by something that thou hast not wrong’d.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Now, by the world—

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: ’Tis full of thy foul wrongs.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: My father’s death—

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Thy life hath that dishonour’d.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Then, by myself—

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Thyself thyself misusest.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Why then, by God—

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: God’s wrong is most of all.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: If thou hadst fear’d to break an oath by Him,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The unity the king thy brother made

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Had not been broken, nor my brother slain:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: If thou hadst fear’d to break an oath by Him,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The imperial metal, circling now thy brow,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Had graced the tender temples of my child,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And both the princes had been breathing here,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Which now, two tender playfellows to dust,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Thy broken faith hath made a prey for worms.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: What canst thou swear by now?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The time to come.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: That thou hast wronged in the time o’erpast;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: For I myself have many tears to wash

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Hereafter time, for time past wrong’d by thee.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The children live, whose parents thou hast slaughter’d,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Ungovern’d youth, to wail it in their age;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: The parents live, whose children thou hast butcher’d,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Old wither’d plants, to wail it with their age.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Swear not by time to come; for that thou hast

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Misused ere used, by time misused o’erpast.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: As I intend to prosper and repent,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: So thrive I in my dangerous attempt

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Of hostile arms! myself myself confound!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Heaven and fortune bar me happy hours!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Day, yield me not thy light; nor, night, thy rest!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Be opposite all planets of good luck

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To my proceedings, if, with pure heart’s love,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Immaculate devotion, holy thoughts,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I tender not thy beauteous princely daughter!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: In her consists my happiness and thine;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Without her, follows to this land and me,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To thee, herself, and many a Christian soul,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Death, desolation, ruin and decay:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: It cannot be avoided but by this;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: It will not be avoided but by this.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Therefore, good mother—I must can you so—

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Be the attorney of my love to her:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Plead what I will be, not what I have been;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Not my deserts, but what I will deserve:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Urge the necessity and state of times,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And be not peevish-fond in great designs.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Shall I be tempted of the devil thus?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Ay, if the devil tempt thee to do good.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Shall I forget myself to be myself?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Ay, if yourself’s remembrance wrong yourself.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: But thou didst kill my children.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: But in your daughter’s womb I bury them:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Where in that nest of spicery they shall breed

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Selves of themselves, to your recomforture.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: Shall I go win my daughter to thy will?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And be a happy mother by the deed.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: I go. Write to me very shortly,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ELIZABETH
lines.text: And you shall understand from me her mind.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Bear her my true love’s kiss; and so, farewell.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Relenting fool, and shallow, changing woman!

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: My gracious sovereign, on the western coast

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Rideth a puissant navy; to the shore

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Throng many doubtful hollow-hearted friends,

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Unarm’d, and unresolved to beat them back:

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: ’Tis thought that Richmond is their admiral;

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: And there they hull, expecting but the aid

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Of Buckingham to welcome them ashore.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Some light-foot friend post to the Duke of Norfolk:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Ratcliff, thyself, or Catesby; where is he?

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: Here, my lord.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Fly to the duke: To Ratcliff. Post thou to Salisbury:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: When thou comest thither—To Catesby. Dull, unmindful villain,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Why stand’st thou still, and go’st not to the duke?

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: First, mighty sovereign, let me know your mind,

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: What from your grace I shall deliver to him.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: O, true, good Catesby: bid him levy straight

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The greatest strength and power he can make,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And meet me presently at Salisbury.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: I go.

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: What is’t your highness’ pleasure I shall do

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: At Salisbury?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Why, what wouldst thou do there before I go?

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Your highness told me I should post before.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: My mind is changed, sir, my mind is changed.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: None good, my lord, to please you with the hearing;

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Nor none so bad, but it may well be told.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Hoyday, a riddle! neither good nor bad!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Why dost thou run so many mile about,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: When thou mayst tell thy tale a nearer way?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Once more, what news?

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Richmond is on the seas.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: There let him sink, and be the seas on him!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: White-liver’d runagate, what doth he there?

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: I know not, mighty sovereign, but by guess.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Well, sir, as you guess, as you guess?

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Stirr’d up by Dorset, Buckingham, and Ely,

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: He makes for England, there to claim the crown.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Is the chair empty? is the sword unsway’d?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Is the king dead? the empire unpossess’d?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: What heir of York is there alive but we?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And who is England’s king but great York’s heir?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Then, tell me, what doth he upon the sea?

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Unless for that, my liege, I cannot guess.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Unless for that he comes to be your liege,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: You cannot guess wherefore the Welshman comes.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Thou wilt revolt, and fly to him, I fear.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: No, mighty liege; therefore mistrust me not.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Where is thy power, then, to beat him back?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Where are thy tenants and thy followers?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Are they not now upon the western shore,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Safe-conducting the rebels from their ships!

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: No, my good lord, my friends are in the north.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Cold friends to Richard: what do they in the north,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: When they should serve their sovereign in the west?

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: They have not been commanded, mighty sovereign:

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Please it your majesty to give me leave,

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: I’ll muster up my friends, and meet your grace

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Where and what time your majesty shall please.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Ay, ay, thou wouldst be gone to join with Richmond:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I will not trust you, sir.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: Most mighty sovereign,

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: You have no cause to hold my friendship doubtful:

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: I never was nor never will be false.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Well,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Go muster men; but, hear you, leave behind

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Your son, George Stanley: look your faith be firm,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Or else his head’s assurance is but frail.

lines.speaker: STANLEY
lines.text: So deal with him as I prove true to you.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: My gracious sovereign, now in Devonshire,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: As I by friends am well advertised,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Sir Edward Courtney, and the haughty prelate

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Bishop of Exeter, his brother there,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: With many more confederates, are in arms.

lines.speaker: SECOND MESSENGER
lines.text: My liege, in Kent the Guildfords are in arms;

lines.speaker: SECOND MESSENGER
lines.text: And every hour more competitors

lines.speaker: SECOND MESSENGER
lines.text: Flock to their aid, and still their power increaseth.

lines.speaker: THIRD MESSENGER
lines.text: My lord, the army of the Duke of Buckingham—

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Out on you, owls! nothing but songs of death? He striketh him.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Take that, until thou bring me better news.

lines.speaker: THIRD MESSENGER
lines.text: The news I have to tell your majesty

lines.speaker: THIRD MESSENGER
lines.text: Is, that by sudden floods and fall of waters,

lines.speaker: THIRD MESSENGER
lines.text: Buckingham’s army is dispersed and scatter’d;

lines.speaker: THIRD MESSENGER
lines.text: And he himself wander’d away alone,

lines.speaker: THIRD MESSENGER
lines.text: No man knows whither.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I cry thee mercy:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: There is my purse to cure that blow of thine.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Hath any well-advised friend proclaim’d

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Reward to him that brings the traitor in?

lines.speaker: THIRD MESSENGER
lines.text: Such proclamation hath been made, my liege.

lines.speaker: FOURTH MESSENGER
lines.text: Sir Thomas Lovel and Lord Marquis Dorset,

lines.speaker: FOURTH MESSENGER
lines.text: ’Tis said, my liege, in Yorkshire are in arms.

lines.speaker: FOURTH MESSENGER
lines.text: Yet this good comfort bring I to your grace,

lines.speaker: FOURTH MESSENGER
lines.text: The Breton navy is dispersed by tempest:

lines.speaker: FOURTH MESSENGER
lines.text: Richmond, in Yorkshire, sent out a boat

lines.speaker: FOURTH MESSENGER
lines.text: Unto the shore, to ask those on the banks

lines.speaker: FOURTH MESSENGER
lines.text: If they were his assistants, yea or no;

lines.speaker: FOURTH MESSENGER
lines.text: Who answer’d him, they came from Buckingham

lines.speaker: FOURTH MESSENGER
lines.text: Upon his party: he, mistrusting them,

lines.speaker: FOURTH MESSENGER
lines.text: Hoisted sail and made away for Brittany.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: March on, march on, since we are up in arms;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: If not to fight with foreign enemies,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Yet to beat down these rebels here at home.

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: My liege, the Duke of Buckingham is taken;

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: That is the best news: that the Earl of Richmond

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: Is with a mighty power landed at Milford,

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: Is colder tidings, yet they must be told.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Away towards Salisbury! while we reason here,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: A royal battle might be won and lost:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Some one take order Buckingham be brought

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To Salisbury; the rest march on with me.

stage_directions.text: Enter Queen Elizabeth and the Duchess of York.
Enter King Richard, marching, with drums and trumpets.
How now, what news with you?

glosses.definition: prosperity starts to ripen (and rot) — Margaret's fruit-image for Richard's now-overripe tyranny
stealthily have I lurked — Margaret has remained in England (contrary to the actual historical timeline; she died 1482) as a prophetic presence
grim prologue — 'induction' = theatrical opening section; Margaret explicitly names the play's structure
buds not yet opened — Elizabeth's dead children
fixed in eternal judgement (damned or saved forever)
one right cancels another — Margaret's calculus of retributive justice
one Plantagenet pays back another — the dynasty self-consuming
the Duchess's formal paradox-string — an oxymoron-lament
already owed to the grave, still walking — the Duchess as living corpse
lawful English ground — for a ceremonial sitting
seniority; priority of ancient grief

glosses.phrase: I had an Edward, till a Richard kill’d him
glosses.definition: Margaret's systematic arithmetic of the dead — the scene's most famous formal structure (anaphora + parallel syntax)
the Duchess's womb as a dog-kennel that bred Richard — Margaret's brutal image

glosses.phrase: dog, that had his teeth before his eyes
glosses.definition: an allusion to the legend that Richard was born with teeth (cf. 2.4.28) — Margaret repurposes it into monstrosity
sore, inflamed eyes — reflecting Richard's reign of sorrow
God as righteous arranger — the 'justice-disposing' theology Margaret enacts
fleshly dog — Richard
church-bench companion — Margaret makes the Duchess a fellow-mourner

glosses.phrase: Young York he is but boot
glosses.definition: the younger prince is but 'boot' (a supplement, the makeweight in a trade)
adulterous Hastings — Hastings' affair with Jane Shore, invoked as his undoing
hell's black spy/agent — Richard as Satan's informant on earth
only kept as their (hell's) agent
cancel his life-contract — Margaret's legal-accounting theology
Margaret's repeat signature-insult for Richard (cf. 1.3.239)
Margaret's repeat (cf. 1.3.238) — Elizabeth is now the painted queen Margaret foretold

glosses.phrase: flattering index of a direful pageant
glosses.definition: the deceptively-attractive table-of-contents of a dreadful show — index = introductory matter

glosses.phrase: mother only mock’d with two sweet babes
glosses.definition: a mother mocked (by fate) with two sweet babes — now dead
mock-queen, queen in play
recite all this grammatically (as one declines a Latin noun through its cases) — Margaret makes grief a grammar-exercise
wretch crowned with anxieties
the wheel of Fortune/Justice has come full circle
Margaret accuses Elizabeth of having taken her throne — now both are queens of sorrow

glosses.phrase: English woes will make me smile in France
glosses.definition: Margaret's exit-line — her grief-schadenfreude

glosses.phrase: dull; O, quicken them with thine
glosses.definition: my words are sluggish; sharpen them with yours — Elizabeth asks Margaret for the craft of cursing
wind-filled advocates — words as lawyers for grief (Queen Elizabeth's formal definition)
inheritors of joys that died without a will — grief as unlegal heirs (intestate = died without a will)
be abundant in outcries — stage direction in dialogue
the Lord's anointed king — Richard claims biblical protection (Psalm 105:15)
peevish and willful
the peak years of adulthood
obscure topical joke — possibly a local personality; Richard's flippant deflection

glosses.phrase: tire thee more / Than all the complete armour
glosses.definition: weigh you down more than full armor — the Duchess's curse as spiritual armor-weight
Elizabeth would rather her daughters enter convents than become Richard's pawns
cheated by their uncle — pun on cousin/cozen

glosses.phrase: poor bark, of sails and tackling reft
glosses.definition: poor boat stripped of sails and rigging — Elizabeth as wrecked vessel
the Lethe (river of forgetfulness) of your angry soul — Richard asks Elizabeth to forget
Elizabeth's ghastly dowry-suggestion: give her daughter the princes' hearts
the handkerchief Margaret mockingly gave York at Wakefield — soaked in young Rutland's blood; Elizabeth proposes its symmetry
swift transport — Elizabeth's euphemism for Anne's murder

glosses.phrase: ruins of distressful times / Repair’d with double riches
glosses.definition: the wreckage of the past made whole with doubled wealth — Richard's salesman's promise
turned to eastern pearl — tears as precious jewels; eastern pearls were the most valued

glosses.phrase: sweet silent hours of marriage joys
glosses.definition: Richard's sales pitch reaches sexual innuendo — addressed, grotesquely, to the girl's mother
Caesar of Caesar — above all emperors; Richard's ultimate imperial flattery
don't keep playing that note — don't keep returning to the children

glosses.phrase: by my George, my garter, and my crown
glosses.definition: by the Order of the Garter's St George jewel, the Garter itself, and the royal crown — the three great insignia Richard swears by

glosses.phrase: thrive I in my dangerous attempt
glosses.definition: Richard's self-conditional curse — 'may I thrive in the battle only if I am sincere'
may I destroy myself — ironic self-curse (which the ghost scene will honour)
powerful fleet — Ratcliff's report of Richmond's invasion
Richmond commands the fleet
drift with sails furled — awaiting news from shore
'Hoyday' = an exclamation of surprise; Richard's sarcasm
cowardly deserter — 'white-livered' = lacking blood in the liver, i.e. cowardly

glosses.phrase: the chair empty? is the sword unsway’d?
glosses.definition: is the throne vacant? is the sword without a wielder? — Richard's sarcastic protest that he is still king

glosses.phrase: leave behind / Your son, George Stanley
glosses.definition: hostage-taking: Stanley's son kept to ensure his father's loyalty — historically accurate
a Devon Yorkist-turned-Tudor ally; historical figure
Richard's fury at messenger after messenger — owls = birds of ill omen
Milford Haven, Wales — Richmond's landing point, 7 August 1485

annotations.title: The three women together — the play's formal peak
annotations.body: The first hundred lines of 4.4 are the play's most intricately constructed formal set-piece, and one of the densest in Shakespeare. Queen Margaret, Queen Elizabeth, and the Duchess of York arrange themselves in a triangle of grief; each has lost an Edward, each has lost a second male kinsman, each occupies a specific position in the moral ledger the play has been keeping. The central passage — "I had an Edward, till a Richard kill'd him; / I had a Harry, till a Richard kill'd him: / Thou hadst an Edward, till a Richard kill'd him; / Thou hadst a Richard, till a Richard killed him" — uses near-identical syntax to enact precise moral arithmetic. Every grief rhymes with every other; every murder is paid against every other. The form is Senecan antiphony refined through medieval complaint-poetry and brought to a level Shakespeare himself will rarely exceed. When Margaret and Elizabeth together review the dead ("The adulterate Hastings, Rivers, Vaughan, Grey"), they are also reviewing Margaret's 1.3 curse fulfilled.
sources: Nicholas Brooke, Shakespeare's Early Tragedies (1968)
Wolfgang Clemen, A Commentary on Shakespeare's Richard III (1968)
Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

annotations.title: Margaret returns — the chorus collects its dividends
annotations.body: Margaret's soliloquy — "So, now prosperity begins to mellow / And drop into the rotten mouth of death" — is one of the most savage re-entries in Shakespeare. She has remained in England (historically impossible — she died in France in 1482) to witness each of her 1.3 curses being fulfilled, and now she enters to watch the last convulsions. "A dire induction am I witness to" — the vocabulary is Senecan, the posture choric. The scene's later line-for-line grief arithmetic is Margaret's grim exam: Elizabeth and the Duchess must now admit, in her presence, what they could not accept in 1.3. The play is asking whether the victim of yesterday's crime owes moral witness to today's victims when she was herself dismissed. Margaret is in one reading heartless and in another — closer to the play's own — terrible precisely because she is the only one left with the historical memory to be accurate.
sources: Patricia-Ann Lee, "Reflections of Power: Margaret of Anjou and the Dark Side of Queenship" (1986)
Phyllis Rackin, Stages of History (1990)
A. P. Rossiter, Angel with Horns (1961)

annotations.title: Elizabeth becomes Margaret's pupil
annotations.body: Elizabeth's request — "O thou well skill'd in curses, stay awhile, / And teach me how to curse mine enemies!" — is the play's cleanest reversal. In 1.3 Elizabeth had dismissed Margaret as a "lunatic" (1.3.251). Now she asks Margaret to teach her the craft. Margaret's answer is methodologically specific: forbear sleep, fast, remember dead happiness against living woe, "Compare dead happiness with living woe; / Think that thy babes were fairer than they were, / And he that slew them fouler than he is." The curse is a daily discipline of recollection. What Margaret is teaching is what she has become: someone whose grief survives because she refuses to let time soften it. The scene is the play's rarest gift — a moment of instruction across a victim-solidarity that crosses the Lancaster-York line. It will not prevent any of what comes, but it changes Elizabeth into someone capable of resisting Richard in the second half of the scene.
sources: Madonne M. Miner, "'Neither mother, wife, nor England's queen'" (1980)
Janet Adelman, Suffocating Mothers (1992)
Kathryn Schwarz, Tough Love: Amazon Encounters in the English Renaissance (2000)

annotations.title: The Duchess of York's maternal curse
annotations.body: The Duchess's farewell to Richard — "take with thee my most heavy curse; / Which, in the day of battle, tire thee more / Than all the complete armour that thou wear'st!" — is the play's most terrible maternal curse. She has traced his whole life in a single speech — grievous burthen at birth, tetchy infancy, frightful schooldays, venturous manhood, treacherous confirmed age — and concludes that no comfortable hour graced her in his company. The curse's specific invocation is that his own mother's prayers will fight for his adversary in the battle: "My prayers on the adverse party fight." The play's supernatural machinery has now added the mother's prayer to Margaret's curse-chain and the murdered souls' ghost-summons. In 5.3 all three will converge against Richard in the tent. The Duchess is one of the ghosts of the battle before the battle begins.
sources: Janet Adelman, Suffocating Mothers (1992)
Coppélia Kahn, Man's Estate: Masculine Identity in Shakespeare (1981)
Phyllis Rackin, Stages of History (1990)

annotations.title: The second wooing — and the question whether Elizabeth yields
annotations.body: Richard's attempt to woo Elizabeth of York through her mother is the play's structural answer to 1.2. The scene is roughly ninety lines, again stichomythic, again a duel of wits — but this time the woman opposing Richard is not exhausted and widowed; she is the mother of the murdered princes and has just been instructed by Margaret in the craft of curse. Critics remain divided on whether Elizabeth "yields" at the scene's end. The surface text ("I go. Write to me very shortly, / And you shall understand from me her mind") can be read as capitulation (Richard thinks so: "Relenting fool, and shallow, changing woman!"), as tactical delay (she knows Richmond is approaching), or as outright deceit (she is simultaneously sending Derby a letter arranging Elizabeth of York's marriage to Richmond — confirmed in 4.5). Modern productions mostly favour the tactical-deceit reading; the wordplay's aggressiveness supports it. What is clear is that Richard misjudges: the 1.2 method does not work on a mother who has read the play's earlier scenes.
sources: Madonne M. Miner, "'Neither mother, wife, nor England's queen'" (1980)
Janet Adelman, Suffocating Mothers (1992)
Marjorie Garber, Shakespeare and Modern Culture (2008)
Harriet Hawkins, "The Authority of Tradition, the Authority of Experience" (1978)

annotations.title: "Swear then by something that thou hast not wrong'd"
annotations.body: Elizabeth's systematic demolition of Richard's oaths (364–397) is one of the great rhetorical sequences in Shakespeare. He swears by his George, his garter, and his crown: profaned, dishonour'd, usurped. The world: full of his wrongs. His father's death: his life has dishonour'd it. Himself: misused. God: most wronged of all. Her method is not counter-argument but exclusion — the method of someone who has realized that Richard's rhetoric only works where a remainder of uncompromised value is available. She takes away every possible guarantor of his promise. The speech's closing thrust — "Swear not by time to come; for that thou hast / Misused ere used, by time misused o'erpast" — is the play's most precise articulation of the logic Margaret had been teaching: the future is already mortgaged by the past. This is the play's one entirely successful rhetorical duel against Richard. He loses.
sources: Brian Vickers, The Artistry of Shakespeare's Prose (1968)
Madonne M. Miner, "'Neither mother, wife, nor England's queen'" (1980)
Joel B. Altman, The Tudor Play of Mind (1978)

annotations.title: "Relenting fool, and shallow, changing woman!"
annotations.body: Richard's aside after Elizabeth exits — "Relenting fool, and shallow, changing woman!" — is the scene's trap for him. He thinks he has won; the play lets us see that he has not. The coming scenes will reveal that Elizabeth has sent word to Derby that "the queen hath heartily consented / He shall espouse Elizabeth her daughter" — meaning Richmond, not Richard (4.5.17–18). Richard's contemptuous summary of her is the index of his misreading, and in a play whose hero is defined by his reading of others, being wrong about a woman is the first indication that his command of the scene has slipped. Margaret's curse — "Thy friends suspect for traitors while thou livest, / And take deep traitors for thy dearest friends" (1.3.219–220) — is fulfilled here in miniature. Richard cannot tell who he is talking to anymore.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)
Madonne M. Miner, "'Neither mother, wife, nor England's queen'" (1980)

annotations.title: "Kennel of thy womb" — Margaret's extreme maternal curse
annotations.body: Margaret's image of the Duchess's womb as "kennel" to a "hell-hound" — "a dog, that had his teeth before his eyes, / To worry lambs and lap their gentle blood" — is the play's most extreme treatment of its maternal-monstrous register. The compressed allusion to the birth-with-teeth legend (cf. 2.4, 1.3's "rooting hog") is weaponized here into a vision of the Duchess as unwitting kennel for a hunting-dog that hunts its own family. It is almost impossible maternal language. What justifies it dramaturgically is that the Duchess herself has been moving toward this language since 2.2 ("from my dugs he drew not this deceit") and 4.1 ("cockatrice hast thou hatch'd"). Margaret names what the Duchess has already been confessing. The scene's moral economy requires that the curse come from the outsider-queen rather than the mother, but the mother must consent. When the Duchess says "God witness with me, I have wept for thine" (59), she is consenting to Margaret's reading of her own son.
sources: Janet Adelman, Suffocating Mothers (1992)
Phyllis Rackin, Stages of History (1990)
Madonne M. Miner, "'Neither mother, wife, nor England's queen'" (1980)

trials.prompt: The central passage "I had an Edward, till a Richard kill'd him..." does what formally?
options: It uses unstructured exclamation to convey grief
It uses near-identical syntactic parallelism to enact precise moral arithmetic — every grief rhymes with every other
It introduces new characters by their titles
It is a quotation from an Old English lament poem

trials.prompt: What is the structural function of Margaret's return in 4.4, given that historically she had died in 1482?
options: To provide comic relief before the final act
To serve as the play's chorus — her historical impossibility as deliberate dramaturgy, keeping the cross-dynastic moral ledger present at the scene of its fulfillment
To introduce a romantic subplot
To represent France's official political position

trials.prompt: Elizabeth's "O thou well skill'd in curses, stay awhile, / And teach me how to curse mine enemies!" marks what change?
options: She has become confused about political allegiances
She explicitly becomes Margaret's pupil — the reversal from her 1.3 dismissal of Margaret as a lunatic
She is preparing a spell for use against Richmond
She is mocking Margaret to distract her from grief

trials.prompt: The Duchess of York's curse on Richard includes the line "My prayers on the adverse party fight." What does she invoke?
options: The military chaplain of Richmond's army
Her maternal prayers fighting for Richmond — the mother's prayer added to Margaret's curse-chain and the murdered souls' ghost-summons that will all converge in 5.3
A specific saint she believes will intercede
An ancient family motto

trials.prompt: Most modern critics read Elizabeth's exit in the second wooing (4.4) as what?
options: A genuine capitulation, as Richard himself believes
Tactical delay or outright deceit — she is in fact arranging her daughter's marriage to Richmond, not Richard (confirmed in 4.5)
A permanent break with all political engagement
A conversion to a religious order

trials.prompt: Elizabeth's systematic demolition of Richard's oaths (364–397) succeeds by what method?
options: Direct counter-argument on each point
Exclusion — she takes away every possible uncompromised guarantor of his promise (his George, his garter, the world, his father's death, himself, God), leaving nothing uncorrupted to swear by
Appeal to a higher authority in heaven
A legal argument citing specific statutes

trials.prompt: Richard's aside at 427–428 — "Relenting fool, and shallow, changing woman!" — marks what?
options: The moment of Richard's final triumph
The scene's trap for Richard: he thinks he has won; the audience sees (and 4.5 will confirm) that he has been outmanoeuvred
A transitional soliloquy about his next military move
A reflection on his mother's character

trials.prompt: Margaret's "kennel of thy womb" image compresses which motif recurring since 2.4?
options: The boar as Richard's heraldic badge
The folk legend that Richard was born with teeth — weaponized here into a vision of the Duchess's womb as unwitting kennel for a hunting-dog
The biblical motif of the lion and the lamb
The classical topos of the basilisk's gaze
